1: Come, let us return to the LORD; he has torn us, but he will heal us; he has wounded us, but he will bind up our wounds — chapter 6 opens with what appears to be a penitential psalm; many scholars identify it as Israel’s shallow repentance that YHWH’s response in Hosea 6:4-6 rejects. The logic of Hosea 6:1-3 (“he has torn us, but he will heal us”) moves too quickly from judgment to restoration without addressing the sin that caused the judgment. The same YHWH who was the lion (Hosea 5:14) and moth (Hosea 5:12) is now expected to immediately restore — but without the “acknowledgment of guilt” specified in Hosea 5:15. This premature confidence in divine healing without genuine repentance is what YHWH finds insufficient.
2: After two days he will revive us; on the third day he will raise us up, that we may live before him — the “two days… third day” formula is a common Hebrew idiom expressing a short, definite period (cf. Exodus 19:11; Genesis 22:4; 2 Kings 20:5). Israel expects a speedy divine rescue after the Assyrian defeat. In its original context this is not predictive prophecy about Christ’s resurrection but reflects the penitent’s assumption that YHWH will quickly restore. However, the availability of this language in the OT canonical context is part of what Paul may mean by Christ rising “on the third day in accordance with the Scriptures” (1 Corinthians 15:4) — the resurrection fulfills the pattern of YHWH raising up his people, definitively and personally.
3: Let us press on to know the LORD; his appearing is as certain as the dawn; he will come to us like the rain, like the spring rain that waters the earth — Israel compares YHWH’s coming to the agricultural calendar’s most reliable events: the dawn and the seasonal rains. The yoreh (early rain, autumn) and malkosh (spring rain) were the agricultural year’s defining events, absolutely regular in the climate of Canaan. The problem YHWH identifies in Hosea 6:4 is that Israel treats his coming with the same predictable routine as the natural rains — a covenant relationship reduced to impersonal natural process, indistinguishable from the Baal cult it replaced.
4: What shall I do with you, Ephraim? What shall I do with you, Judah? Your love is like a morning cloud, like the early dew that goes away — YHWH’s first-person lament; the double “what shall I do with you?” expresses divine frustration rather than divine confusion. Morning cloud and early dew are images of the most transient phenomena in the Palestinian climate: the morning cloud burns off in the heat and the dew evaporates within hours. Israel’s hesed (covenant loyalty) is as ephemeral as its own morning mist. The irony: in Hosea 6:3, Israel praised YHWH’s coming as certain as the dawn; now YHWH uses dawn imagery to describe Israel’s love as insubstantial as morning vapor.
5: Therefore I have hewn them by the prophets; I have slain them by the words of my mouth, and my judgment goes out as light — the prophetic word is YHWH’s primary instrument of discipline: the prophets have been cutting (chatsavti, hewed as one hews stone or wood) Israel with their oracles. The image positions prophetic proclamation as surgical: painful but purposeful, cutting away the corrupted tissue. My judgment goes out as light — the transparency and certainty of YHWH’s verdict; in contrast to the morning cloud that disappears, YHWH’s judgment shines clearly and does not evaporate.
6: For I desire covenant loyalty (hesed) and not sacrifice; the knowledge of God (daʿat ʾelohim) rather than burnt offerings — the programmatic statement of prophetic theology: the priority of relational covenant faithfulness over cultic performance. This is not the abolition of sacrifice but the prophetic insistence that sacrifice without hesed is meaningless or counterproductive. The same principle appears in Samuel (“to obey is better than sacrifice,” 1 Samuel 15:22), Isaiah (“I cannot endure iniquity and solemn assembly,” Isaiah 1:13), Amos (Hosea 5:21-24), and Micah (Hosea 6:6-8). Jesus quotes Hosea 6:6 twice — Matthew 9:13 (defending eating with sinners) and Matthew 12:7 (defending Sabbath grain-plucking) — making it the interpretive key to his critique of Pharisaic legalism.
7: But like Adam they have transgressed the covenant; there they have dealt faithlessly with me — the reference to Adam (keʾadam) is either “like Adam” (the individual) or “like mankind/human beings” or “at Adam” (a place in the Jordan valley; cf. Joshua 3:16). If the referent is the individual Adam, this may be the earliest explicit prophetic comparison of Israel’s covenant sin to the primordial human covenant violation in Eden. The sin of eating the forbidden fruit is the paradigm for Israel’s transgression of the Sinai covenant: both involve clear divine instruction, deliberate violation, and the forfeiting of the blessed conditions that obedience would have maintained.
8: Gilead is a city of evildoers, stained with blood — Gilead, the Transjordanian territory east of the Jordan, had been a site of significant political violence in the later monarchy: the assassination of Zechariah son of Jeroboam II (2 Kings 15:10, at Ibleam, near Gilead) and subsequent political instability. The description of a city (“Gilead is a city of evildoers”) may be a collective term for the region’s population or may refer to a specific city (Ramoth-gilead or another Gileadite center) known for its violence.
9: As raiders lie in wait for someone, so do bands of priests; they murder on the road to Shechem and commit the worst of crimes — Shechem was both a levitical city and a city of refuge (Joshua 20:7; Hosea 21:21), where those guilty of manslaughter could find asylum. The irony is extreme: the priests who are supposed to provide sanctuary at the refuge city are instead ambushing travelers on the road to it. The priestly office has been completely inverted: instead of offering life, it takes it. This is the logical outcome of Hosea 4:8 (priests feeding on sin offerings — benefiting from iniquity).
10: I have seen a horrible thing in the house of Israel: there Ephraim’s prostitution is found; Israel is defiled — shaʿaruryah (“horrible thing”) is one of the strongest Hebrew terms of revulsion: something hair-raising, shocking to the moral sensibility. The word appears in Jeremiah 5:30 and 23:14 for the condition of the false prophets and Jerusalem; it signals a situation that has gone so far beyond normal corruption that it produces visceral horror. The naming of “the house of Israel” as the location of this horror contrasts bitterly with what the “house of Israel” was supposed to be — YHWH’s dwelling place among his people.
11: Also, O Judah, a harvest is appointed for you, when I restore the fortunes of my people — the dual message of the chapter’s final verse: Judah will also face a harvest of judgment (the coming judgment, whether Assyrian or Babylonian), and yet the phrase shavti shevut ammi (“when I restore the fortunes of my people”) is the standard phrase of eschatological restoration (Jeremiah 30:3; Ezekiel 39:25; Joel 3:1; Amos 9:14). The verse holds both judgment and hope simultaneously: the harvest Judah will reap is a judgment harvest, but the same frame of “restoring fortunes” that the prophets use for the great eschatological renewal is already present as the horizon toward which even this judgment is moving.
